text: HeforShe: Bargaining Power, Parental Beliefs, and Parental Investmentarah Walker r○ 1, Pauline Grosjean r○ 1, Alejandrina Cristia r○ 3, and Adeline Delavande r○ 4 1 School of Economics, UNSW Sydney 2 CEPR 3 Laboratoire de Sciences Cognitives et de Psycholinguistique, ENS; EHESS; CNRS; PSL University 4 Economics Department, University of Technology Sydney; Nova School of Business and Economics January 28, 2025 Abstract We estimate a structural model of intra-household allocation of parental investments in children.

We combine novel measures of verbal investments and beliefs about the returns to investment for both mothers and fathers with hyper-local variation in female bargaining power stemming from ancestral inheritance practices.
Female bargaining power influences the quantity and quality of paternal investments.
Specifically, fathers speak more to children, and in a more engaging way, when women have more bargaining power, but only when their wife expects investments to improve child language.
Our results single out the role of economic power in mothers’ ability to elicit domain-specific paternal investment.
Policy simulations show large increases in paternal investments from a female empowerment policy.
Keywords: Parental investments, Child-directed speech, Female bargaining power, Parental beliefs.

JEL Codes: D13, O15, Z13 HeforShe is the United Nations Global Solidarity Movement For Gender Equality. “It is an invitation for men and people of all genders to stand in solidarity with women to create a bold, visible and united force for gender equality.
The men of HeForShe aren’t on the sidelines.
They’re working with women and with each other to build businesses, raise families, and give back to their communities.” (https://www.heforshe.org/en).
We extend our deepest gratitude to the communities in Western and Choiseul provinces for welcoming us and sharing their time with the research team.

The authors’ names have been randomized using a uniform distribution on the American Economic Association website (confirmation code: 7WnkPZH0wEpn).
Parental investments during early childhood constitute a major determinant of child human capital development (see e.g.
List et al., Attanasio et al., and García and Heckman for overviews of the literature).

In particular, a rich language environment in the early years is essential for young children’s vocabulary and language acquisition, as well as their long-term development, spurring recent interventions to foster parental verbal investments.
However, the existing literature primarily focuses on maternal investments, overlooking both the influence of paternal investments and the intrahousehold allocation of these investments.
Worldwide, over 93% of all rigorously-evaluated early parenting interventions have focused on supporting mothers exclusively, even though over 70% of households with children have a father present.
This paper studies the intra-household allocation of investments in children by both parents.

Our analysis rests upon a unique trifecta: (i) frontier measures of mother’s and father’s investments, (ii) data on beliefs held by both parents about the returns and costs of these investments, and (iii) external variation in women’s bargaining power stemming from deeply rooted cultural factors.
A key innovation is our measure of parental investments – speech.
We measure maternal and paternal speech from child-centered day-long recordings among children aged 6 months to 4 years (26 months, on average).
Day-long recordings were collected using a small device fitted to the child’s clothing and then analyzed with a Voice Type Classifier (VTC, Lavechin et al. ), an end-to-end neural network.

This approach allows us to capture three important dimensions of investment by each parent: the overall quantity of speech, the quantity of child-directed speech, and the quality of child-directed speech, as proxied by the pitch of the parent’s voice while talking to the child. 1 Motivated by the evidence on the role of beliefs about the technology of child development in shaping parental investments (e.g., Cunha et al.; Boneva and Rauh; Attanasio et al.; List et al.; Bhalotra et al. ), we elicit both parents’ beliefs about the production function of language development, as well as their perceived costs of talking to children. 2 In addition, our field setting, the Solomon Islands, offers extremely rich variation in traditional cultural practices even among otherwise ethno-linguistically ho Emotional expressiveness, particularly higher pitch, is key in maintaining a child’s attention and fostering social and cognitive engagement (see e.g., Saint-Georges et al.; Ramírez et al. ). 2 We are only aware of the work of Almås et al. who elicit beliefs from fathers as well as mothers about the importance of certain parental inputs in Tanzania.
mogeneous communities.
This enables us to leverage hyper-local variation in female bargaining power based on matrilineal inheritance, in which daughters inherit land from their mothers.

We provide evidence that matrilineal inheritance is a reliable proxy of female bargaining power, with women enjoying more agency and leisure, even among otherwise similar villages. 3 We also rely on other cultural determinants of female bargaining power, such as bride price (see, e.g., Anderson; Ashraf et al.; Lowes and Nunn ).
We begin our analysis by modeling intra-household allocation of parental speech investments.
We assume a collective household model in which the mother’s (Pareto) weight in the collective utility function is higher under a traditionally matrilineal inheritance structure.
Both parents derive utility from child language development but bear a cost associated with speech investments.
Parents have imperfect information about the language production function and form individual-specific beliefs about the return of their investment.
The model provides insights into how expected returns, perceived cost of investment, and female bargaining power influence paternal investments in children.
Specifically, it predicts that both the father’s investment and the elasticity of his investment with respect to the mother’s expected returns increase with the mother’s Pareto weight.
This latter prediction, which captures the subtle interplay between beliefs and bargaining dynamics beyond the simple effect of inheritance practices, is the main focus of our analysis.
We structurally estimate the parameters of the model to test its predictions and perform counterfactual analysis.
Empirically, we document large gaps in investments between parents.
Mothers talk twice as much to children and elevate pitch more when talking to children, indicating more intense engagement during communication.
Consistent with the model’s predictions, fathers in matrilineal villages speak more than fathers in patrilineal villages.
We also find that both parents report positive returns, of similar magnitudes, and positive costs of investments.
Yet, the findings reveal distinct pathways for paternal and maternal investments.
Fathers are more responsive to their perceived costs of investment, while mothers respond primarily to their expected returns.

Our main result is that women who expect a higher return are able to elicit more paternal speech investment when they have greater bargaining power: the elasticity of paternal investments to mother’s expected returns increases in female bargaining power.
A one standard deviation increase in mothers’ beliefs about the returns to parental speech increases fathers’ speech investments by 0. standard deviations in matrilineal villages.

This result holds for several measures of speech input and for quality, and is consistent with the ethnographic 3 See also, e.g La Ferrara; Gneezy et al. and Lowes for evidence on the connection between matrilineal inheritance and female status.
record showing that women in societies with matrilineal inheritance have greater leverage to demand higher paternal investment (see e.g.

Turning to mechanisms, we find that while husbands of more powerful women do spend more time taking care of children, they only invest more in speech when mothers have high expected returns to child-directed speech.
This illustrates how the quantity and quality of paternal investments are specific to the domains where mothers expect high returns.
Further, we show that the additional paternal investment is primarily directed towards girls, in line with maternal preferences over child gender.
We rule out several other channels that could explain the relationship between maternal beliefs and father speech in matrilineal villages, including assortative matching and learning.

In particular, we show that husbands of highreturns mothers in matrilineal villages are observationally similar to others, including in terms of beliefs and cost of speech, education, income sources, and social preferences.
Highreturns mothers in matrilineal villages, as well as their children, are also similar to others.
Finally, we pinpoint the role of economic power in driving paternal investment as opposed to other potential dimensions of female status, including kin and family networks due to residence patterns.
We provide additional evidence on the influence of individual economic power by leveraging quasi-random variation in the death of the mother’s parents and within-village variation in bride price.
We discuss the quality of our probabilistic beliefs data and analyze the sensitivity of our results to specification choices.

We show that our results are robust to different assumptions about the functional form of the relationship between Pareto weights and matriliny, as well as different assumptions about the cost function.

In particular, our results are insensitive to the set of conditioning variables at the child-, parental-, householdor village-level that enter the cost function, as well as considering altruistic individuals who internalize their spouse’s cost in their utility function.
Our results are also robust to different assumptions about the structure of the error terms.
Our paper contributes to the economics of parenting.

Parents play a crucial role in shaping children’s early development (e.g., List et al. ), and there is a growing body of literature examining the determinants of parental investments in children (see Cunha and Heckman for an early contribution and Doepke et al. and Doepke and Zilibotti for recent reviews).
However, the literature on parenting offers only limited evidence on fathers’ investments.

Most theoretical models only consider a single parent and a single child (see discussion in Doepke et al. ), and empirical studies predominantly examine mothers. 4 Our contribution is to analyze investments made by both mothers and fathers, as 4 See Evans and Jakiela for a recent survey of the emerging literature on fathers’ involvement in low and middle-income countries, and Doepke and Zilibotti for data on parenting styles from both well as the dynamic of intra-household allocation of these investments.

We achieve this through a novel approach, combining a frontier and objective measure of investment that is consistent across parents, with precise data on both parents’ beliefs regarding investment returns and the associated costs.

A basic identification problem in understanding the drivers of parental investment is that observed investments are consistent with many alternative specifications of preferences, expected returns, perceived costs, and bargaining power.
Our data allow us to disentangle their respective roles and shed new light on how female bargaining power affects paternal investments.

This data collection effort answers recent calls by e.g., Almås et al. and Caplin (forthcoming) for the measurement of typically unobserved constructs in order to improve inference on decision-making without overly restrictive assumptions.
This paper also makes several contributions to the literature on the importance of social structures for the status of women and economic development (e.g.
Greif; Alesina and Giuliano; Henrich; Bahrami-Rad et al. ).
While most previous evidence highlights how social structures affect group-level outcomes, our approach and data microfound such aggregate relationships.
We provide the first application of a structural model to understand how deeply rooted cultural variables affect decision making.

We model social structures as a bargaining power shifter and we exploit rich variation in traditional inheritance, residence, and marriage patterns that teases out various dimensions of female status and isolates the role of economic bargaining power.

Related literature connects female-biased cultural practices, such as matrilineal inheritance, matrilocal residence, and bride price to female bargaining power, female autonomy and status, as well as parental investments in children.
However, this literature typically leverages variation at the ethnicity level.
We instead rely on within-ethnicity variation, attenuating potential concerns that variation in specific practices confounds other cultural or institutional differences across ethnic groups.
Our focus on female bargaining power contributes to the literature on women’s empowerment and its implications for intra-household decisions toward children.
Earlier studies indicate that female bargaining power is associated with higher spending and better nutrition for children (e.g., Thomas; Lundberg et al.; Duflo ).
We show how female bargaining power also shapes the allocation of non-financial, time-intensive investments in children. mothers and fathers in high-income countries.
See also Giannola for an example of how parents allocate resources between siblings.
Our findings have several implications for the design of policy interventions.
Early childhood development interventions can have substantial impacts on children’s cognitive and socioemotional development, but these interventions so far overwhelmingly rest on mothers.

Given that time allocation over household activities and child rearing is already a major dimension of gender inequality even in the most advanced economies, these interventions have recently spurred concerns about exacerbating gender inequalities by further restricting female labor force participation and imposing additional childcare commitments on mothers.
Yet, so far, with only scant evidence on fathers’ investments and decision-making, involving fathers in early childhood programs has proven challenging.
We begin to fill this gap in the literature.
Our policy simulations suggest that female empowerment policies are the most effective at increasing paternal investments in child development.

For instance, an intervention equivalent to transitioning from fully patrilineal to equal inheritance leads to a 17% increase in fathers’ verbal engagement with their children, significantly outpacing the impact of alternative interventions.
Additionally, reducing fathers’ costs of engaging with their children—such as through playgroups that teach enjoyable activities—modestly increases fathers’ vocalizations (by 1–2%).
By contrast, information interventions targeting fathers’ beliefs about the benefits of verbal engagement yield no measurable effects.
However, informing mothers about these benefits not only increases their own verbal engagement but also generates positive spillovers to fathers.
Combining increasing women empowerment and expected returns with reducing father’s cost leads to a 21–22% increase in paternal vocalizations in patrilineal settings and 6–8% in matrilineal ones.
Our analysis and results, overall, highlight the importance of measuring potential policy spillovers across parents. 5
We present a simple model of intra-household allocation of parental investment showing the role of bargaining power, parental expectations about child development, and parental cost of investment.

We build on the seminal collective model of Chiappori, which starts from the basic assumption that in a multi-person household, household members have their own utility functions but cooperate in a bargaining process that results in a Pareto-efficient allocation.
We consider a household with a mother m, a father f and one child c.

Each parent i derives utility from the child’s human capital, proxied by language development Θ, and 5 See Guo et al. for evidence of spillover effects from an early childhood development program, where untreated children benefited through their parents’ social connections to families receiving the intervention.
incurs a cost represented by a twice-differentiable convex cost function C i ( x i ) associated with their time-intensive investment x i.
Parent i ’s utility is given by:
U i ( x i, − C i ( x i ), i = m, f where ω i is parent i ’s utility associated with the child’s language development.

Looking at the household decision-making, we assume a collective model in which the household utility function is a weighted sum of the mother’s and father’s expected utility function, U m and U f respectively:
U = αU m + (1 − ’s utility in the couple’s utility function.
In our context, α is assumed to vary with the inheritance structure in the village where the couple lives.
The key characteristic of the collective model is that household decisions are efficient.
This efficiency is achieved by maximizing the household utility function U subject to household constraints.
As standard in these models, we assume common knowledge of individual preferences and expectations.
The household faces a language production function constraint. 6 The objective production function of child c ’s language is as follows:
’s human capital endowment at birth, x is the total investment received by the child, 7 and ’s and mother’s speech inputs are perfect substitutes.

However, as we discuss below, the assumption of perfect substitutability is not key to our results. 8 Existing work typically assumes linearity, CES or Cobb-Douglas production function (e.g., Cunha et al.; Tincani et al. ).
parameters describing returns to investment.
The production function that parent i perceives is given by: ’s expected utility is based on their own perceived production function, formulated as:
E U i (( x i, − C i ( x i ), i The couple will choose the investment levels x f and x m to maximise the household expected utility subject to two effort constraints x i, i = m, f.
The expected household utility function is given by:
E U = − C m ( x m ) ] + (1 − − C f ( x f ) ]. Assuming an interior solution, the first-order-conditions with respect to x m and x f are given by:

− C ′ m ( x m )) + (1 − − − C ′ f ( x f ) Our model first establishes that the optimal investment strategy of a parent is increasing in both their and their spouse’s expected returns ’s Pareto weight α (and vice versa for mothers).
We are also interested in how the father’s investment x f responds to changes in the mother’s belief parameter
− α ) C ′′ f ( x f ). (9) Thus, the elasticity of the father’s investment to the mother’s beliefs ’s expected return when she has higher bargaining power.

In Section 4, we estimate the structural parameters of the model, leveraging unique data on parental investments x f and x m, parental beliefs ’ decision-making process over their respective time-intensive investment in children and the role of bargaining power and beliefs.

The simplifying assumption that parental investments are perfect substitutes is not key to our results, as long as the beliefs about the elasticities of child language development to own and spouse’s investments are positively correlated.
Our model omits other potentially important aspects, such as multidimensional skills, the role of financial inputs, the child’s agency, and labor force participation.
These simplifications are suited to our context.

As we discuss in more detail in the next section, our focus is on early childhood (6 to 48 months), where the child has yet no agency and speech development is the primordial skill, and in a subsistence economy context, where opportunities for financial investments in children and formal employment are limited.
Our study site is located in the Solomon Islands, in the South Pacific, which comprises six major islands and over 900 smaller ones.
The people of the Solomon Islands are Melanesian, and display great cultural diversity, with 72 distinct living languages (according to Ethnologue ) for a total population of around 800,000 people.
The Solomon Islands ranks 156th out of 193 countries on the Human Development Index, reflecting ongoing challenges in health, education, and living standards (United Nations Development Programme ).

We conducted our data collection from June to August 2019 in 44 villages across two provinces, Western and Choiseul. 9 The villages in our sample are small, isolated, coastal lowland communities that depend mainly on subsistence horticulture and fishing for their livelihoods.
Only 4.85% of respondents in our sample derive any income from paid work.
On average, people in our sample work in their gardens a couple of times a week and sell goods in the market less than once a month.
In addition, men fish a couple of times a week and women a few times a month.
An average village consists of 87 households and 441 people, most of whom have no access to grid electricity, running water, or improved sanitation.

Descriptive statistics in Table A1 show that electricity is “used by most households” in only 7% of villages and only 3% of households have access to running water. 10 Only half of the households have a water tank, and only 25% of the villages always have enough drinking water for all households.
The main mode of transport is by ship or outboard canoe and, in general, access to roads is extremely limited.

The average travel time between the villages in the sample and the provincial capital is six and half hours, and the average travel time to the country’s capital city Honiara, on the island of Guadalcanal, is two and a half days, leaving most communities isolated from major market centers.
Through public lotteries in each village, we randomly selected a total of 820 adults.
A subset of 182 married couples with at least one child aged 6 months to 4 years old, for whom we surveyed both the wife and the husband, constitutes our analytical sample.

In a few cases in which the parents had more than one child within the target age range, we collected information on the additional children, for a total of 196 children. 11 The average age of the children in our sample is 26 months, the median 24 months, and 98.15% of children are less than 3 years old.
Mothers are 29 years old on average, and fathers, 33 years old.
Appendix E provides further details on the sample and survey data.
We conducted individual surveys with both parents, including a dictator game and competition task, and recorded the target child over the course of an entire day.
We collected day-long child-centered recordings using a wearable device, an increasingly used technique in early language development research.

In our study, 9 There were two survey teams in Western and one in Choiseul, each consisting of two research assistants and two local enumerators, with at least one female and one male, who could speak English, Pijin (the lingua franca), and at least one local language.

The composition of the teams did not change over the course of the data collection. 10 We take access to a flush toilet as our proxy of access to running water. 75% of households use the bush, the river or the sea as their “main type of toilet”. 11 We check that our results are invariant to dropping the 14 additional children, or to clustering standard errors at the household level to account for correlated observations across siblings.
each child wore a t-shirt fitted with two small breast pockets, into which a pair of USB voice recorders were inserted.
Two recorders were used because some devices may stop functioning in field conditions with high humidity and variable temperatures.

We provide more information on the recording technology in Appendix Section C. Parents were informed that the study aimed to explore how children acquire language and assured that the content of their conversations would not be listened to.
We obtained 377 recordings for 196 children.
Compliance was very high, with only one family not consenting to the recording, and recordings lasting 15. hours on average (SD 4. hours, range 0. minutes - 24 hours).
Less than 5% of the recordings lasted fewer than 6 hours, and this is similar by matriliny (see Appendix Table A2 ).
We focus on early childhood as a critical time for language acquisition.
Young children are particularly sensitive to language stimulation, the investment we focus on.
Any shortfall at this stage can have lasting effects due to the brain’s remarkable plasticity.
Differences in early conditions often become self-reinforcing due to dynamic complementarity with subsequent investments.
In addition, our focus on early childhood facilitates a cleaner analysis by limiting child agency and the type of parenting decisions (such as parenting styles, e.g., Doepke and Zilibotti ).
Language acquisition is a particularly central element of cultural transmission in the Solomon Islands, where children are expected to learn several languages from an early age.
For the very young children in our sample, language acquisition is the most central skill.

Beyond language diversity, the Solomon Islands also have important oral traditions that are transmitted to children and include origin stories, genealogies, songs, customary knowledge, reminiscences about the past, and spells and prayers, including many dating from the preChristian era.

section_name: Matrilineal inheritance in the Solomon Islands
text: Recent anthropological research suggests that cultural diversity was a defining feature of pre-contact small scale societies (see, e.g.

section_name: Matrilineal inheritance in the Solomon Islands
text: Diamond; Graeber and Wengrow ).

section_name: Matrilineal inheritance in the Solomon Islands
text: Anthropologist Marshall Sahlins, reflecting on the Pacific Islands, noted how “the native peoples of Pacific Islands unwittingly present to anthropologists a generous scientific gift: an extended series of experiments in cultural adaptation”. The Solomon Islands, in particular, provide a context of uniquely rich and hyper-local variation in land inheritance practices, even within ethno-linguistic cultural groups that are otherwise similar in their family structure and cultural characteristics.

section_name: Matrilineal inheritance in the Solomon Islands
text: The persistence of cultural diversity in the Solomon Islands owes to a large extent to the limited reach of the centralized state.

section_name: Matrilineal inheritance in the Solomon Islands
text: In matrilineal villages of the Solomon Islands, daughters inherit land from their mothers.

section_name: Matrilineal inheritance in the Solomon Islands
text: This form of matrilineal land inheritance is the norm in matrilineal societies of the Pacific – with genetic, linguistic and archaeological evidence establishing the ancestral prevalence of matriliny dating as far back as 5,000-4,500 BP – as well as in some societies of southern and central Africa and some native American cultures. 12 Today, nearly 90% of the land is still owned customarily, vested either in matrilineal or patrilineal clans and tribes (Stege, 2008; United Nations Development Programme (UNDP), 2021).

section_name: Matrilineal inheritance in the Solomon Islands
text: Matrilineal inheritance is combined with significant responsibility of women over land and substantial female empowerment. 13 A given inheritance system is dominant within a village but may coexist with another system.

section_name: Matrilineal inheritance in the Solomon Islands
text: Accordingly, we measure matrilineal bias as a categorical variable, M h, which takes a value of 0 in exclusively patrilineal villages (41.5% of our sampled villages), 0. in villages where patrilineal inheritance is dominant but not exclusive (26.8%), 0. in villages where matrilineal and patrilineal inheritance are equally prevalent (7.3%), 0. in villages where matrilineal inheritance is dominant but not exclusive (14.6%) and 1 in exclusively matrilineal villages (9.8%). 14 The mean value of M h is 0.30, with a standard deviation of 0.36.

section_name: Matrilineal inheritance in the Solomon Islands
text: In our sample, women in matrilineal villages are more likely to have inherited land (Panel A of Table 1 ) and, consistent with the literature on the positive consequences of matriliny for female bargaining power and outcomes, they enjoy more leisure time and have later ages at first marriage (by 1. years).

section_name: Matrilineal inheritance in the Solomon Islands
text: Women in matrilineal villages keep 10 percentage points more of the dictator game endowment when they play with their spouse, compared to women in patrilineal villages, an indication of a greater economic power within the household (Gnagey et al. ). 15 We also observe that the distribution of household assets is tilted towards female preferences in matrilineal villages.

section_name: Matrilineal inheritance in the Solomon Islands
text: Households in matrilineal 12 In other matrilineal cultures, such as the ones studied by La Ferrara, land is transferred from the mother’s brother to his sister’s son.

section_name: Matrilineal inheritance in the Solomon Islands
text: This is prevalent in areas that practice avunculocal residence, where a male child is expected to join his maternal uncle’s residence.

section_name: Matrilineal inheritance in the Solomon Islands
text: Avunculocal residence is a minority practice in matrilineal systems and is absent in our sample.

section_name: Matrilineal inheritance in the Solomon Islands
text: Consequently, in our sample, uncles are hardly involved in their sisters’ children’s lives, even less so than unrelated individuals, with no difference by matriliny. 13 As stated by a woman of nearby Palau island, “the women here are so sure of themselves. maybe it’s that we know for sure that we have land.

section_name: Matrilineal inheritance in the Solomon Islands
text: Even if I don’t get land from my husband, I still have it from my mother and nothing can change that.” (cited in Stege ). 14 The matrilineal bias variable codes the village leader’s answers to the question: “How is land inherited in this village?” - (i) father; (ii) mother; (iii) both, but mostly father; (iv) both, but mostly mother; (v) both, mother and father equally; (vi) other.

section_name: Matrilineal inheritance in the Solomon Islands
text: We check the robustness of our results to coding M h as a categorical variable.

section_name: Matrilineal inheritance in the Solomon Islands
text: See in particular Figure 2. 15 We provide more detail on all survey and experimental measures in Section E.

section_name: Matrilineal inheritance in the Solomon Islands
text: villages are more likely to own a water tank and a toilet, but there is no difference in ownership of a mobile phone, boat, radio, or solar lamp.

section_name: Matrilineal inheritance in the Solomon Islands
text: Importantly, our data confirms that matrilineal and patrilineal villages are otherwise comparable in cultural and political organization (Table 1 ).

section_name: Matrilineal inheritance in the Solomon Islands
text: We observe no differences in the participation of women vs. men in formal village governance structures nor generalized trust levels.

section_name: Matrilineal inheritance in the Solomon Islands
text: We also observe no difference in spouses’ tendency to compete with one another, a measure of spousal cooperation, suggesting that household harmony does not vary with matriliny in our context.

section_name: Matrilineal inheritance in the Solomon Islands
text: There is also no statistically significant relationship between fertility and matrilineal inheritance. 16 3.3

section_name: Expected returns and costs of child-directed speech
text: We measure both mothers’ and fathers’ beliefs about the returns to speaking to children with respect to children’s outcomes.

section_name: Expected returns and costs of child-directed speech
text: Specifically, we elicit the subjective probabilities associated with a child achieving a language development milestone in two hypothetical scenarios: one where the caregiver engages in frequent communication (high investment) and another where communication is infrequent (low investment).

section_name: Expected returns and costs of child-directed speech
text: We employ a visual aid approach commonly used in developing countries.

section_name: Expected returns and costs of child-directed speech
text: Respondents are provided with 10 beans to express their beliefs in a probabilistic format, where each bean represents one chance out of 10.

section_name: Expected returns and costs of child-directed speech
text: We adopt the language milestone utilized by Bhalotra et al., specifically, the expectation that a child can string together 2-3 words by the age of 2.

section_name: Expected returns and costs of child-directed speech
text: Data Appendix E details the exact wording and Appendix B. discusses the data in more detail.

section_name: Expected returns and costs of child-directed speech
text: Appendix Figure A1 shows the distribution of parental beliefs for high and low investments.

section_name: Expected returns and costs of child-directed speech
text: There is considerable heterogeneity in beliefs.

section_name: Expected returns and costs of child-directed speech
text: Overall, parents expect a higher probability of a child speaking 2-3 words by age 2 under the high investment scenario.

section_name: Expected returns and costs of child-directed speech
text: The mean answer is 7 for both mothers and fathers in the high-investment scenario and 3 in the low-investment scenario.

section_name: Expected returns and costs of child-directed speech
text: In the US context, 72% of children speak partial sentences by age 2, suggesting that our respondents’ average expectations are well calibrated.

section_name: Expected returns and costs of child-directed speech
text: We present some data quality checks in Appendix B. We transform the data into expected returns, taking the difference in expected outcomes between the high and low investment cases at the individual level. 17 Men and women expect, on average, that frequently talking to the child, as opposed to rarely, will increase the 16 In our sample, when considering the total number of children of women aged 18 to 45 (inclusive) as an outcome, controlling for age and province fixed effects, the point estimate associated with M h is -0.108 with a standard error corrected for clustering at village level of 0.256 (P-value: 0.673). 17 If parental investments are perceived as perfect substitutes, the expected return is a measure of η i, 2 in 5.

section_name: Expected returns and costs of child-directed speech
text: If parental investments are perceived as imperfect substitutes, the expected return is a weighted average of the subjective elasticities of father’s and mother’s investments in the child language production function.

section_name: Expected returns and costs of child-directed speech
text: likelihood of the child speaking sentences by age 2 by 43 percentage points.

section_name: Expected returns and costs of child-directed speech
text: The distribution of expected returns for men and women is, on average, similar (p-value of 0.483 on a Kolmogorov-Smirnov test of equality of distributions shown in panel (a) of Figure 1 ).

section_name: Expected returns and costs of child-directed speech
text: We also examine the distribution of parental expected returns within a couple.

section_name: Expected returns and costs of child-directed speech
text: This is important because, if parental beliefs were perfectly correlated with one another, we would be unable to measure their respective influences on parental investment or the influence of spousal bargaining power.

section_name: Expected returns and costs of child-directed speech
text: Correlation of parental returns could either result from assortative matching on beliefs, spousal communication and persuasion, or learning from the same environment (including from their child’s specific ability).

section_name: Expected returns and costs of child-directed speech
text: Our data reveals that spouses have distinct beliefs about returns, with a raw correlation of -0.004.

section_name: Expected returns and costs of child-directed speech
text: Panel (c) of Figure 1 shows the complete distribution of couples according to each partner’s return.

section_name: Expected returns and costs of child-directed speech
text: Very few couples are along the 45-degree line, where the distribution would fall if beliefs were perfectly correlated.

section_name: Expected returns and costs of child-directed speech
text: There is no extra mass in the top right quadrant (high returns mothers matched with high returns fathers) nor in the bottom left quadrant (low returns matched to one another).

section_name: Expected returns and costs of child-directed speech
text: We elicit mothers’ and fathers’ perceived cost of talking to their child on a scale from 0 to 10 (see Appendix E ).

section_name: Expected returns and costs of child-directed speech
text: Perceived costs are slightly higher for mothers compared to fathers (p-value of 0.005 on a Kolmogorov-Smirnov test of equality of distributions, see Panel (b) of Figure 1 ).

section_name: Expected returns and costs of child-directed speech
text: Spousal correlation in costs of speech is slightly higher than for returns, but still low (0.209) (see also the distribution of spousal costs in Panel (d) in Figure 1 ).

section_name: Expected returns and costs of child-directed speech
text: Consistent with the absence of other cultural and institutional differences, parental beliefs about returns and costs are similar across matrilineal and patrilineal villages, as shown in Table B1.

section_name: Expected returns and costs of child-directed speech
text: We also observe no differences by matriliny in the absolute gaps between mothers’ and fathers’ expected returns and costs (Table A3 ).

section_name: Expected returns and costs of child-directed speech
text: A small absolute gap suggests assortative matching on beliefs and costs, or effective spousal communication concerning these factors.

section_name: Expected returns and costs of child-directed speech
text: Thus, our findings indicate no variation in assortative matching or spousal communication based on inheritance patterns.

section_name: Speech as a measure of parental investments
text: Speech recordings were analyzed with a Voice Type Classifier (VTC, Lavechin et al. ), an end-to-end neural network trained with a combination of various child-centered corpora of children aged 0-4 years in multilingual environments, including languages spoken in the Pacific, which match the conditions of our study. 18 A precursor of this method, called LENA, has been used for over a decade in the USA and in a recent study of an information intervention targeting mothers in urban Ghana 18 Twelve different languages are spoken across our sample.

section_name: Speech as a measure of parental investments
text: Children grow up in multilingual environments, including at least one local language and the lingua franca.

section_name: Speech as a measure of parental investments
text: (Dupas et al., forthcoming).

section_name: Speech as a measure of parental investments
text: Our method relies on more recent neural network architectures, which allow a more accurate classification of female and male voices. 19 In Appendix Section C, we discuss how VTC compares with alternative technologies, including manual annotations.

section_name: Speech as a measure of parental investments
text: For every 10 ms frame, VTC returns whether the key child (i.e., the child wearing the recording device), another child, an adult female, or an adult male is vocalizing.

section_name: Speech as a measure of parental investments
text: A vocalization is then defined as a sequence of frames where the relevant individual vocalizes.

section_name: Speech as a measure of parental investments
text: Following standard practice in the field, we counted the number of vocalizations attributed to the relevant speaker over the whole recording.

section_name: Speech as a measure of parental investments
text: We then divided that by the length of the recording and re-scaled to 12 hours to produce a per day figure that accounts for 12 hours of sleep.

section_name: Speech as a measure of parental investments
text: Our main outcomes of interest to measure parental investments are the resulting normalized adult female and male vocalizations per day. 20 These vocalizations may include both speech directed at the child and speech overheard by the child.

section_name: Speech as a measure of parental investments
text: Yet, existing literature has shown that the quantity of adult vocalizations, regardless of whether they are directed at the child or not, is positively correlated with children’s production of speech-like vocalizations, suggesting that overall adult speech is a relevant input into child language development. 21 Additionally, recognizing the importance of child-directed speech and high-quality interactions for child development (e.g., Weisleder and Fernald; Suskind ), we use two supplementary measures of the quantity of child-directed speech: conversational turns and vocalizations at high pitch.

section_name: Speech as a measure of parental investments
text: To capture the quality of child-directed interactions, we use a measure of pitch when talking to children.

section_name: Speech as a measure of parental investments
text: Child-directed speech, often called motherese or parentese, is characterized by higher pitch, a pattern that is near-universal according to a recent systematic review. 22 This type of speech captures young children’s attention and promotes engagement.

section_name: Speech as a measure of parental investments
text: Studies demonstrate that infants respond more positively to high pitch speech, reinforcing parental use and fostering a feedback loop that enhances communication, supporting early language development, turn-taking in conversa F-score is a measure of accuracy that goes from 0 to 100%, higher is better.

section_name: Speech as a measure of parental investments
text: See exact definition in Appendix D. 20 A potential concern with this measure is that it might reflect proximity to the child rather than actual stimulation.

section_name: Speech as a measure of parental investments
text: In our exploration of mechanisms, we show that our results are not mediated by how much time the father spends with the child. 21 In a study involving children aged 2 to 48 months from 12 countries across six continents and varied living contexts, Bergelson et al. find that for every 100 adult vocalizations per hour, children produced 27 additional vocalizations.

section_name: Speech as a measure of parental investments
text: In our data, children produce 52. (30.5) more vocalizations per day for every 100 female (male) vocalizations per day.

section_name: Speech as a measure of parental investments
text: See Appendix Table A4. 22 To our knowledge, the only language in which a higher pitch for child-directed over adult-directed speech has not been observed is Quiche’ Mayan.

section_name: Speech as a measure of parental investments
text: tions, and vocabulary growth (e.g., Saint-Georges et al.; Ramírez et al. ).

section_name: Speech as a measure of parental investments
text: Pitch is also a strong predictor of the emotional connection between caregiver and child. 23 The first measure of child-directed speech, Conversational Turn Count or CTC, is commonly used in studies employing LENA (see e.g.

section_name: Speech as a measure of parental investments
text: Romeo et al., Dupas et al. (forthcoming) and Cunha et al. ).

section_name: Speech as a measure of parental investments
text: CTC tallies child-adult or adult-child vocalization sequences, aiming to capture conversation.

section_name: Speech as a measure of parental investments
text: However, CTC may be a noisy measure, as it may count instances that are not actual conversations, such as a child vocalizing after an adult speaks, regardless of whether the child is responding, interrupting, or simply talking to themselves, a likely event given the young age of children in our sample (6 to 48 months). 24 To improve on the CTC measure and increase the likelihood that we capture a childparent interaction, we analyze the pitch characteristics of adult vocalizations.

section_name: Speech as a measure of parental investments
text: We measure the median pitch of each vocalization within a recording and then calculate the average of these median pitches for each individual.

section_name: Speech as a measure of parental investments
text: Our analysis uses on two pitch-related measures: the total number of vocalizations that exceed the average pitch, as a measure of quantity of child-directed speech; and the average median pitch during conversational turns, as a measure of quality of child-directed speech. 25 The current state of the technology does not make it possible to distinguish further between female or male voices.

section_name: Speech as a measure of parental investments
text: From hereon, we consider female (male) vocalizations as mothers’ (fathers’) vocalizations.

section_name: Speech as a measure of parental investments
text: This approximation may introduce measurement error but is not unreasonable given that children in our sample spend the vast majority of their time with their parents.

section_name: Speech as a measure of parental investments
text: Other than parents, but far behind, children receive the most care from their maternal grandmother and from neighbors and friends, who on average take care of the child between once a week and several times a month in our sample (see Cassar et al. for more detail).

section_name: Speech as a measure of parental investments
text: To deal with such potential measurement error, we account for the frequency of care by female or male individuals other than the parents in some specifications. 26 Descriptive Statistics 23 In Appendix Table A5, we confirm that pitch is higher during a conversational turn with a child. 24 One difference between our measure of CTC and the LENA measure of CTC is that LENA throws out overlapping speech between adult and speech, while our measure counts it as CTC. This explains why mean CTC is higher in our sample compared to, e.g., Dupas et al. (forthcoming). 25 Pitch is therefore missing if no conversational turn occurs during the recording.

section_name: Speech as a measure of parental investments
text: This is true for 6 recordings, all of which have a duration of less than one minute. 26 We will also show in columns 12 to 14 of Table A9 that care by people other than the parents is unrelated to mothers’ expected returns, matriliny and their interaction.

section_name: Speech as a measure of parental investments
text: Potential measurement errors therefore would be orthogonal to our main variables of interest.
Our objective is to estimate the optimal mother and father investments given by equations ( 6 ) and ( 7 ).
To ensure tractability, we impose several parametric assumptions.

First, we assume that the convex cost function is given by C i ( x i ) = % level. 28 The Solomon Islands displays great linguistic diversity, with 12 different languages spoken across the villages in our sample.
in perceived returns and given by:
x − − − − − − − ’s Pareto weight α is increasing in matrilineal inheritance prevalence.
Fourth, we assume there is an additive measurement error in our parental speech measures.
This error is assumed to be normally distributed with a mean of zero.

Since the measurements of maternal and paternal investment are derived from the same recording, we allow for the possibility that the measurement errors may be correlated between maternal and paternal investments.

We test the robustness of our estimates to alternative parametric assumptions in Appendix Section B. In particular, we allow for spousal altruism and for different functional form assumptions for the relationship between matrilineal inheritance and female bargaining power, namely relaxing the linearity assumption.
We estimate the optimal parental investment using Seemingly Unrelated Regressions:
Speech m,c,h = a m + ’s beliefs about the return to speaking to children.
M h measures the prevalence of matrilineal inheritance (hereafter, matriliny) in the village.
C i is the reported perceived 29 We replace M h = 0. 1 when M h = 0 to take the ratio.
cost of talking to children, while the vector X i,c,h captures how the cost of talking to children varies with parent, spouse, child, and household level characteristics as described above.
These characteristics include the child’s age and gender.
To capture how the cost may be influenced by cognitive ability, we control for i ’s performance on a cognitive task similar to solving Raven’s matrices.

Given previous literature’s findings about the importance of socioeconomic conditions for parental investments (e.g., Cunha et al.; Boneva and Rauh ), we include an asset-based index of wealth of the household. 30 We also include the number of children in the household, which may influence how much time a parent may have to devote to the recorded child, and for whether the child is the father’s biological father. 31 We analyze the sensitivity of our results to different specifications of the cost function.
We omit the province subscript for simplicity, but we control for a set of province fixed effects throughout our analysis.
Speech was recorded by two USBs for each child, as insurance against the possible technical defects of a single recorder.

To account for multiple recordings, we cluster standard errors at the child level in our baseline specifications, but consider alternative group-level structures of the error terms at the household or village level.
Different USBs also had different performances due to random technical or accidental factors, so that the duration of recording varies across observations.

We expect this heterogeneity to affect the precision of our estimates, so we weight each observation by the recording duration of each USB. Key to our identification strategy is the heterogeneity in expected returns within couples.

Without this variation, it would be impossible to disentangle the role of individual beliefs from those of their spouses, and consequently, to understand how bargaining power shapes the elasticity of investment to spousal beliefs.
We can identify the cost parameters ’s shutdown.
preferences for child development (scaled by the positive cost parameter).
The parameters ’s investment to mother’s beliefs varies with women bargaining power.
Finally, we also expect κ 1, which measures how the perceived cost of talking to children, influences the investment, to be negative.

We first document basic differences in investments across mothers and fathers by estimating a simpler version of Equations ( 19 ) and ( 20 ) in which we only consider how total vocalizations per day vary based on own beliefs and costs parameters.
The results are presented in column 1 of Table 3 (top panel for mothers and bottom panel for fathers).
The results reveal an asymmetric pattern across genders.

For mothers, only the coefficient associated with their expected beliefs about the returns of speech is statistically significant, while for fathers, only the coefficient associated with their self-reported direct cost of speech is statistically significant. 32 The coefficient associated with expected beliefs (“ Returns ”) measures ’ investments are more elastic to beliefs, while fathers’ investment are more elastic to direct costs.
For mothers, a one standard deviation increase in returns is associated with a 0. standard deviation increase in vocalizations.
For fathers, a one standard deviation decrease in direct costs is associated with a 0. standard deviation increase in vocalizations.
Our model also predicts that parental investment increases with their spouse’s bargaining power.

While we do not observe a statistically significant effect of male bargaining power on maternal investment (the coefficient on 1/Matriliny is positive but imprecisely estimated), we find a substantial effect of female bargaining power on fathers’ total vocalizations per day.
Specifically, transitioning from a fully patrilineal to a fully matrilineal system increases fathers’ vocalizations by 0. standard deviations.
This result aligns with the descriptive evidence presented in Table 2.
Column 2 of Table 3 augments column 1 by considering the role of spouse’s expected returns.
The coefficients associated with spouse’s returns are small and statistically insignificant, both for mothers and fathers.

Spousal beliefs about the returns to parental speech thus do not influence individual investment, on average. 32 The difference between the coefficients associated with maternal and paternal beliefs is statistically significant at the 12% level.
The difference in the costs coefficients is statistically significant at the 7% level.
Columns 3 presents the estimation results of Equation ( 19 ) and ( 20 ) that matches our structural model.
We focus on the elasticities of parental investments to their spouse’s expected return.
For mothers, the coefficients associated with fathers’ returns are insignificant, regardless of bargaining power.
The model predicts that when fathers care about language development, they will leverage their bargaining power to induce more investments by mothers.

Yet, we have found evidence that fathers’ investments are not elastic to their own returns, which suggests that fathers have low preferences for language development. 33 It is therefore not surprising that maternal investments do not respond to fathers’ beliefs: even when fathers have high bargaining power, they do not seek to elicit maternal investments in the particular domain studied in this paper.

For fathers’ investments (column 3, bottom panel), the coefficient on the interaction between mothers’ expected returns and matriliny—which captures the elasticity of fathers’ investment to mothers’ beliefs in contexts where women have high bargaining power—is positive and statistically significant at the 5% level.

This finding is consistent with our theoretical prediction that when women have greater bargaining power, their beliefs about the returns to investment will have a stronger influence on fathers’ investments.
The effect size is large.

A one standard deviation increase in mothers’ returns is associated with a 0. standard deviation increase in fathers’ speech in matrilineal villages. 34 Since we consistently find a positive and significant coefficient associated with the elasticity of maternal investments to mothers’ returns, this additional result suggests that mothers have a strong preference for language development and leverage their bargaining power to elicit higher investment by their husbands, when they believe parental investments are more productive.
For more flexibility, column 4 augments column 3’s specification to allow for own preference and direct cost parameters to vary by bargaining power shifter.
The results are similar.

If anything, the main coefficient of interest associated with the interaction between matriliny and mothers’ returns in driving paternal speech investments is larger in magnitude and more precisely estimated.

As shown in column 1 of Table B5, the results are also similar in magnitude and statistical significance when we do not include any parent, spouse, child, and household level characteristics as cost shifters.
Tables A13 and A14 display all the coefficients associated with all the included cost shifters.

Since the evidence thus far suggests that maternal investments are only driven by maternal returns, we focus hereon on paternal investment and how it varies by matrilineal 33 To be precise, this evidence is consistent with a low
Nevertheless, given that our model also confirms that measurement errors are correlated across maternal and paternal investments, 35 we maintain our joint estimation strategy.
We now examine other dimensions of paternal investments: the quantity and quality of child-directed speech.
Results are in Table 4.
Even columns display the more flexible specification, as in column 4 of Table 3.
Odd columns omit parent, spouse, child, and household characteristics as cost shifters.

The coefficient associated with the interaction between mothers’ returns and matrilineal inheritance in predicting conversational turns is positive but imprecisely estimated, which may be due to measurement errors in the CTC measure (see discussion in Section 3. ).

We improve on this measure by considering the number of vocalizations at high pitch as an additional proxy of the quantity of child-directed speech in columns 3 and 4, and the pitch during a conversational turn as a measure of the quality of child-directed speech in columns 5 and 6.

The coefficient associated with the interaction between mothers’ returns and matrilineal inheritance is positive and statistically significant at the 5% level in predicting both measures, suggesting high elasticity of paternal quantity and quality of child-directed speech with respect to mother’s returns when she has more bargaining power.

A one standard deviation increase in mothers’ returns is associated with a 0. standard deviation increase in the number of vocalizations at high pitch and a 0. standard deviation increase in fathers’ pitch during a conversational turn in matrilineal villages.
These results suggest that the paternal speech induced by mothers with higher returns in matrilineal villages is directed to the child and is more engaging to the child.
Figure A2 shows evidence of good model fit for both parents and all speech outcomes.
We provide a battery of robustness analyses.

First, we discuss the quality of our probabilistic beliefs data and the robustness of our results to alternative modeling choices for the beliefs and to potential measurement error in Appendix B. In particular, Table B2 demonstrates that our conclusions remain unchanged after excluding the small number of respondents who violate the monotonicity of probabilities in practice questions, incorporating observations with negative returns to investment in speech, excluding observations with missing returns or costs, and addressing potential measurement errors in beliefs by averaging across our primary measure of returns to speech and alternative metrics, following methodologies outlined in Cunha et al. and Bhalotra et al. 35 The f a Breusch-Pagan test of independence in column 4 is 90. (P-value: 0.00) We also illustrate our results in the raw data by plotting the distribution of fathers’ vocalizations according to matrilineal inheritance and mothers’ expected returns.

Figure A3 shows that the median, 25th, and 75th percentiles of fathers’ vocalizations are higher for men married to high returns mothers in matrilineal villages, where we expect the investments by fathers to be higher.

Median vocalizations by fathers married to high returns mothers in matrilineal villages are one standard deviation higher than median vocalizations in any other group (high returns mothers in pa trilineal villages, low returns mothers in matrilineal villages, low returns mothers in patrilineal villages).
The distribution of the raw data thus suggests that our main results are not driven by outliers.

We also show that there is considerable within-village variation in beliefs and costs, alleviating potential concerns that our relationship of interest is driven by clustering of beliefs or costs in certain village types, rather than by parents acting on their beliefs and costs.
Figure A4 depicts a box plot of mothers’ and fathers’ beliefs and costs for each village in our sample ordered by inheritance type (from patrilineal to matrilineal).
The Figure shows considerable within-village variation in beliefs and costs across all types of inheritance practices.
Table A6 shows that there is actually more within-village than between-village variation in the beliefs and costs.
Overall, this alleviates the concern that our results may be driven by unobserved heterogeneity across villages in the distributions of beliefs and costs.

Finally, we find very low correlation between beliefs and cost (0.0126 for mothers and 0.0391 for fathers), which goes against the idea that parents who anticipate higher returns for an investment internalize the cost of the investment and do not view it as costly.
We then consider robustness to alternative assumptions about Pareto weights and about the agents’ utility function.

In particular, we consider different functional forms for the relationship between Pareto weights and matriliny, and we relax the linearity assumption α 1 − − α and M h, including a non-linear logistic specification.
We show that under some of these alternative specifications, the preference parameters are overidentified and we estimate them jointly out of the two optimal investment equations.

Results in Tables B3 and B4 show that the coefficients capturing the elasticity of paternal speech to mother’s return in matriliny are statistically significant for all measures of paternal investments, including conversational turns.
We also make a series of different assumptions about the cost function.
In Table B5, we first show that our results on total vocalizations, vocalizations at high pitch, and average pitch are insensitive to the choice of conditioning variables entering the cost function.
Column 1 shows that the results are almost identical in specifications with no child or parent-level variables entering the cost functions.
Column 2 further shows that all results are robust, and similar in magnitude to our baseline specification (shown in column 3), when we include child level variables only.
In Column 4, we allow for all the factors to enter the cost function differently by matriliny.
The results are unchanged.

As shown in column 5, the results are robust when the cost also accounts for the amount of care that the child receives separately from female and male caretakers other than their parents. 36 To address the possibility that unobserved heterogeneity across villages or linguistic differences may explain our results, we show in column 6 that our results are robust to controlling for a set of 13 language fixed effects.
Our coefficients of interest are robust and close in magnitude to our main estimates.
In column 7, we show that our results are robust to accounting for the possibility that parents’ costs may depend on the day of the recording by conditioning on date fixed effects.
Column 8 shows that our results are unchanged when we include father’s income source in the cost function, which may affect his opportunity cost.
Last, in column 9, we consider vocalizations by children other than the key child in the cost function.
Inputs by other children may crowd out or substitute for parental investments.
Again, our results are unchanged.

Table B6 also shows that our results are robust to considering altruistic individuals who internalize their spouse’s cost of parental speech, and we allow the altruism parameter to vary with matriliny, accounting for the possibility that matriliny may alter spousal altruism.
Last, we consider different assumptions about the structure of the error terms.

To deal with the few cases in which more than one child was recorded in the household, column 1 of Table A7 shows that our results are identical when we cluster standard errors at the household – rather than child – level, accounting for the possible correlation of the error term across siblings in the same household.
In column 2, we revert to clustering standard errors at child level but keep only the child closest to two years old in the estimation sample, in line with our recruitment protocol.
In the case of twins, we randomly drop one child.
The 36 The P-value in Panel B is exactly 0.100.
Considering care by other caretakers also account for the possibility that the recorded vocalizations may not be from the child’s parents.
The results are robust and close in magnitude to our main estimates, suggesting that such potential measurement error does not bear on our results.
coefficient of interest becomes larger in magnitude.

The last column of Table A7 shows that our results are robust when standard errors are clustered at the village level to account for the possibility of a group structure of the error term within matrilineal villages.
The results are more precisely estimated for the quantity of vocalizations and child-directed speech.
We now provide more evidence on the mechanisms through which mothers with more bargaining power are able to elicit paternal investment when they have high returns.
We first show that paternal investment is specifically tailored to the domain in which high bargaining power mothers expect high returns and to their preferences.

We then single out the role of women’s economic bargaining power, as opposed to other potential dimensions of female status and show additional evidence on the role of women’s individual economic bargaining power.

section_name: Mothers’ beliefs as the driver of investment
text: Here, we show that higher (and better) paternal investments are specific to the domain where women perceive such investments as productive and align with women’s child gender preferences.

section_name: Mothers’ beliefs as the driver of investment
text: First, we pinpoint the role of maternal beliefs as opposed to other maternal, paternal and child characteristics that could influence paternal investments.

section_name: Mothers’ beliefs as the driver of investment
text: Beliefs vs. other determinants of investments A potential concern is that maternal beliefs may proxy other variables that may systematically drive paternal speech.

section_name: Mothers’ beliefs as the driver of investment
text: For example, women with higher returns may systematically marry spouses who would speak more to the child anyway (e.g., because they have low cost or high return); or they may have children who are able to elicit more paternal speech, for example, because they are better at engaging in conversations themselves.

section_name: Mothers’ beliefs as the driver of investment
text: Because we identify the parameter of interest through the interaction of beliefs and matriliny, this would only be a concern for our analysis if these potential confounders systematically varied with matriliny.

section_name: Mothers’ beliefs as the driver of investment
text: Yet, as discussed in Section 3., we do not observe any systematic relationship between matrilineal inheritance and the spousal correlations in returns and costs.

section_name: Mothers’ beliefs as the driver of investment
text: We also do not observe any systematic relationship between matrilineal inheritance and spousal correlations in formal education (P-value: 0.48) or cognition (performance on Raven-like matrices, P-value: 0.88).

section_name: Mothers’ beliefs as the driver of investment
text: Moreover, high-returns matrilineal women marry men that are observationally similar to other fathers in terms of returns and costs of child-directed speech, as well as age, formal education, income source, leisure, social preferences, and who they get help from with childcare.

section_name: Mothers’ beliefs as the driver of investment
text: This is shown in Table A8, in which we regress fathers’ characteristics on mothers’ returns, matriliny, and the interaction between matriliny and mothers’ returns.

section_name: Mothers’ beliefs as the driver of investment
text: Similarly, Table A9 shows that the children of high-returns matrilineal women are also observationally similar to other children in terms of BMI and gender (columns 1 and 2), suggesting that child characteristics may not drive our results.

section_name: Mothers’ beliefs as the driver of investment
text: We also find that highreturns matrilineal women themselves are not observationally different from other women, along a wide range of characteristics, such as age, education, income source, leisure, number of children in the household, or social preferences (columns 3 to 11).

section_name: Mothers’ beliefs as the driver of investment
text: Last, their children are not more likely to receive care from grandmothers or non-relatives (as reported both by mothers, as shown in columns 12 to 14 of Table A9, and by fathers, as shown in columns 10 to 12 of Table A8 ).

section_name: Mothers’ beliefs as the driver of investment
text: To gauge the possible influence of other, unobservable dimensions of the match, or of the couple’s child, we perform a randomization inference test, in which we randomly reassign the mother’s returns over 1,000 repetitions.

section_name: Mothers’ beliefs as the driver of investment
text: In other words, we keep all other dimensions of the match between a husband and wife as well as the child’s characteristics constant, and only systematically vary the mother’s returns.

section_name: Mothers’ beliefs as the driver of investment
text: Figure A5 displays the results for all three outcomes of interest, and shows that our estimated coefficients of interest on the interaction between mother’s belief and matriliny are well outside the range of coefficients estimated from the random reassignments.

section_name: Mothers’ beliefs as the driver of investment
text: A last potential concern is that higher beliefs among women may reflect learning by those who place a strong value on child development.

section_name: Mothers’ beliefs as the driver of investment
text: If women with a higher taste for child language development engage in more frequent communication with their children, they might learn that speech has high return.

section_name: Mothers’ beliefs as the driver of investment
text: This could lead to a positive correlation between the unobserved taste and the expected return.

section_name: Mothers’ beliefs as the driver of investment
text: However, our findings do not provide evidence of learning.

section_name: Mothers’ beliefs as the driver of investment
text: Parents with a greater number of children do not exhibit systematically different expected returns (see Table B1 ), and the correlation of returns for parents, who observe the development of the same children, is low (refer to Figure 1 ).

section_name: Mothers’ beliefs as the driver of investment
text: Moreover, to affect our results, learning should be heterogeneous by matriliny, which is unlikely given the absence of differences in mother investment with matriliny. 37 37 Finally, even if the positive coefficient of the interaction between the expected return and matriliny were entirely driven by an omitted taste parameter, our results would indicate similar intrahousehold dynamics to the ones we have described: powerful women who value parental investment can induce greater paternal investment.

section_name: Mothers’ beliefs as the driver of investment
text: General versus specific investments Overall, these additional analyses suggest that paternal investment in matrilineal places is specifically driven by maternal beliefs.

section_name: Mothers’ beliefs as the driver of investment
text: A question, however, is whether such paternal investment is specific to the domain of maternal beliefs, or only trickles down from a higher level of general paternal care, since more powerful women may simply be able to extract more care from the father.

section_name: Mothers’ beliefs as the driver of investment
text: To answer this question, we collected data on father’s time spent on child care, as reported by mothers. 38 Column 1 of Table 5 confirms that, in line with standard models of household bargaining, fathers spend more time taking care of their children in matrilineal villages.

section_name: Mothers’ beliefs as the driver of investment
text: However, as shown in column 2 of Table 5, this measure of overall time investment does not vary with maternal beliefs in matrilineal villages.

section_name: Mothers’ beliefs as the driver of investment
text: In other words, although all fathers spend more time taking care of their children in matrilineal villages, only those partnered with high returns mothers invest more in child-directed speech.

section_name: Mothers’ beliefs as the driver of investment
text: Moreover, we show that all our results on speech investments are robust to conditioning on time spent by fathers taking care of children (Table A10 ).

section_name: Mothers’ beliefs as the driver of investment
text: This further shows that our speech measures capture targeted investments rather than overall care and that paternal investments respond specifically to the domain of maternal beliefs.

section_name: Mothers’ beliefs as the driver of investment
text: Last, we show that paternal investments also align with typical maternal preferences over child gender.

section_name: Mothers’ beliefs as the driver of investment
text: Existing literature typically shows that mothers have balanced preferences for boys and girls, while fathers tend to exhibit son’s preference (e.g.

section_name: Mothers’ beliefs as the driver of investment
text: Dizon-Ross and Jayachandran, Lundberg ). 39 To test which child (by gender) is the recipient of paternal investment, we augment Equation ( 20 ) with a triple interaction of maternal returns, matriliny and female child to allow for the preference parameter for language development to differ by child gender.

section_name: Mothers’ beliefs as the driver of investment
text: The results, shown in columns 3 to 5 of Table 5 show that paternal speech induced by high-beliefs mothers in matrilineal villages is primarily directed toward girls.

section_name: Mothers’ beliefs as the driver of investment
text: The coefficient associated with the triple interaction is positive and precisely estimated for total vocalizations, although less precise for other measures.

section_name: Mothers’ beliefs as the driver of investment
text: Appendix Table A12 shows that the results are also significant for total vocalizations at high pitch when we consider a dummy variable taking value one in purely matrilineal villages. 38 Women are asked: “How often does your husband help you taking care of your children?”. 39 In our sample, mothers display equal preferences for daughters and sons according to measures of maternal investment and preferences such as breastfeeding.

section_name: Mothers’ beliefs as the driver of investment
text: In a regression where the dependent variable is the age at which mothers stopped breastfeeding on child, the coefficient associated with child female gender is 0. (p-value: 0.899).

section_name: Mothers’ beliefs as the driver of investment
text: When interacted with matrilineal inheritance, it is 2. (P-value: 0.397).

section_name: Mothers’ economic power as the driver of investment
text: In this section, we establish that our results are driven by female economic bargaining power, as opposed to other dimensions of female status.

section_name: Mothers’ economic power as the driver of investment
text: Economic power vs. other dimensions of social status Matrilineal inheritance may not just capture economic bargaining power, but also more general power or status of women, which itself could drive our results.

section_name: Mothers’ economic power as the driver of investment
text: To isolate the role of economic power from other dimensions of female status and power, we leverage the fact that our setting displays variation along both inheritance and residence patterns.

section_name: Mothers’ economic power as the driver of investment
text: Under matrilocal residence patterns, newly married couples reside in the bride’s village after marriage (see, e.g.

section_name: Mothers’ economic power as the driver of investment
text: Women have closer access to their kin in matrilocal places and have higher social status, but not necessarily more land or assets that would confer economic bargaining power. 15% of the villages in our sample are matrilocal (with the remaining 85% patrilocal), about half of which are also primarily matrilineal ( M h more than 0.5).

section_name: Mothers’ economic power as the driver of investment
text: This variation enables us to separate the influence of status and kinship networks from economic bargaining power.

section_name: Mothers’ economic power as the driver of investment
text: Column 1 of Table 6 reports the estimation results of Equation ( 20 ) replacing M h by a dummy variable taking value one if the village is matrilocal.

section_name: Mothers’ economic power as the driver of investment
text: In contrast with matrilineal inheritance, we do not observe more father vocalizations in matrilocal villages, even when mothers have high returns: the coefficient associated with the interaction term of interest is negative and statistically insignificant.

section_name: Mothers’ economic power as the driver of investment
text: Moreover, in column 2, we allow female bargaining power to come from both matriliny and matrilocality by conditioning on both matriliny and matrilocality and their interactions with beliefs and costs.

section_name: Mothers’ economic power as the driver of investment
text: We find that only the interaction between matrilineal inheritance and maternal beliefs is significant.

section_name: Mothers’ economic power as the driver of investment
text: Columns 3 to 6 show similar results for the other quantitative and qualitative measures of fathers’ speech investments.

section_name: Mothers’ economic power as the driver of investment
text: Finally, in Table A11, we rely on drivers of female employment, such as the presence of a market, kindergarten, or primary school in the village, 40 which could influence female empowerment across villages.

section_name: Mothers’ economic power as the driver of investment
text: As before, we allow female bargaining power to come from both matriliny and from these additional village characteristics.

section_name: Mothers’ economic power as the driver of investment
text: In every case, the coefficient of interest associated with the interaction between matriliny and maternal beliefs remains statistically significant at the 1 to 5% level and is stable (often, larger) in magnitude.

section_name: Mothers’ economic power as the driver of investment
text: In the last column, we explore the role of the governance structure of the village, which could affect female political empowerment (presence of an elected leader vs. alternative governance 40 The presence of a kindergarten or school could also influence child cognitive and non-cognitive skills as well as child social preferences, as shown by e.g, Heckman; Cappelen et al., and thereby potentially influence parental investments.

section_name: Mothers’ economic power as the driver of investment
text: structures such as hereditary chief, religious leader, big man, or council of elders).
