section_name: Mothers’ economic power as the driver of investment
text: Our main results are again robust and stable in magnitude.

section_name: Mothers’ economic power as the driver of investment
text: Overall, these results suggest that our results are specific to female economic bargaining power, rather than other sources of status or power stemming from the close presence of kin, as the driver of our results.

section_name: Mothers’ economic power as the driver of investment
text: Direct evidence on economic power We now offer direct evidence demonstrating the influence of economic power.

section_name: Mothers’ economic power as the driver of investment
text: First, as discussed in Section 6, we observe that the increase in paternal investments driven by maternal beliefs increases monotonically with the degree of matriliny – i.e., the intensity of female economic bargaining power.

section_name: Mothers’ economic power as the driver of investment
text: Second, we provide evidence on the role of individual female economic power in columns 7 to 12 of Table 6.

section_name: Mothers’ economic power as the driver of investment
text: In columns 7 to 9, we first leverage variation stemming from quasi-random variation in parental death, which generates variation in whether women have already inherited land.

section_name: Mothers’ economic power as the driver of investment
text: We re-estimate Equation ( 19 ) replacing M h by an individual level variable that takes value one if the woman has inherited land.

section_name: Mothers’ economic power as the driver of investment
text: Relying on such individual level variation enables us to augment our specification with village level fixed effects.

section_name: Mothers’ economic power as the driver of investment
text: In other words, we compare, within a village and traditional inheritance structure, women who have high realized economic bargaining power because land has already passed on to them vs. women who have not inherited.

section_name: Mothers’ economic power as the driver of investment
text: The results show that higher quantity of paternal investment is driven by high returns women who have already inherited.

section_name: Mothers’ economic power as the driver of investment
text: In columns 10 to 12, we rely on another proxy of individual female economic bargaining power, which derives from marriage practices.

section_name: Mothers’ economic power as the driver of investment
text: The practice of bride price – where the groom’s family transfers wealth to the bride’s family at marriage – has been associated with higher educational investments in girls and higher bargaining power and more autonomy conferred to women, including in Melanesia and the Solomon Islands specifically. 41 In our sample, 34.23% of women have received a bride price at marriage. 42 The practice of bride price is uncorrelated with the prevalence of matrilineal inheritance at the village level (raw correlation: 0.005) but more prevalent, although not universal, in patrilocal villages (raw correlation: 0.28), consistent with the ethnographic record (Anderson, 2007; Brandl 41 Brandl and Colleran also discusses potential drawbacks of bride price for women, mostly due to male resentment over high payments and higher cost of divorce for men who might find it difficult to save enough for a subsequent bride.

section_name: Mothers’ economic power as the driver of investment
text: Overall, however, they conclude that the ethnographic record is consistent with the benefits to women, in terms of status and economic security, outweighing the potential drawbacks.

section_name: Mothers’ economic power as the driver of investment
text: They also highlight that women themselves desire bride price. 42 For the remaining 63.76% marriages, no exchange of money or goods took place, and in 2.01% of the cases, there was a reciprocal gift exchange.

section_name: Mothers’ economic power as the driver of investment
text: and Colleran, 2024).

section_name: Mothers’ economic power as the driver of investment
text: Importantly, we rely on individual-level variation in bride price, thus comparing women who received a bride price to others in the same village, enabling us to abstract from other potential village-level determinants of female status.

section_name: Mothers’ economic power as the driver of investment
text: Columns 10 to 12 of Table 6 show, consistent with previous literature documenting higher female economic security and bargaining power associated with bride price, that the husbands of high returns women who have received a bride price talk more to their child and do so at a higher average pitch, with the measures of total vocalizations and pitch statistically significant at the 5% level.
We use the estimated structural parameters to simulate the behavioral responses of mothers and fathers to a series of policy interventions targeted at increasing the quantity of speech investment.

These include manipulation of expected returns, perceived cost of talking to children, and bargaining power. 43 The simulations assume full compliance (e.g., all women fully revise their expectations).
The results therefore constitute upper bounds of policy treatment effects.
The estimates are presented in Table 7.

Column 1 shows the baseline distributions of investments predicted by model (column 3 of Table 3 and Table 4 ) in fully matrilineal and fully patrilineal villages before any of the policies is introduced.
We first discuss the impact of information interventions on the benefits of verbal engagement, similar to what is implemented by Dupas et al. (forthcoming) and Cunha et al.

These interventions can be thought of as delivering information about the objective production function (Equation 3 ), in particular about % in fully matrilineal villages, has no effect on male vocalizations in fully patrilineal villages, and has modest positive effects on female vocalizations in either village.
Effect sizes are similar for the number of high-pitched vocalizations.
Overall, shifting maternal beliefs has modest effects on mothers, who already speak a lot, and only affects 43 The policy interventions have minimal impact on pitch, so we focus on quantity.
fathers if mothers have high enough bargaining power.
Column 3 shows that, consistent with our previous results, shifting paternal beliefs has no effect on any of the parents’ verbal investment with children.

We next simulate results of reducing fathers’ costs of talking by 0. of a SD (column 4). 44 Such an intervention could for example consist in having fathers join a play group and learn fun activities to share with their children.
Reducing fathers’ costs is associated with a very modest (1-2%) increase in fathers’ total and high-pitched vocalizations, with a comparable effect size across matrilineal and patrilineal villages.
The next simulation investigates impacts of a female empowerment intervention.
We consider an intervention that is equivalent to shifting fully patrilineal inheritance to equal inheritance ( M h = 0 to M h = 0. 5 ).
As shown in column 5, female empowerment would lead to a 17% increase in fathers’ vocalizations (total and high-pitched), while mothers slightly reduce their investments in patrilineal places.
Overall, the effect of this female empowerment policy on fathers’ investments is much larger than either increasing mother’s returns or decreasing father’s costs.
We then combine the mother’s beliefs, father’s costs, and female empowerment policies in column 6.
This combined intervention increases fathers’ vocalizations by 21 to 22% in patrilineal places and 6 to 8% in matrilineal places.
This paper studies the intra-household allocation of verbal investments in children.
We documents large gaps in speech investments in children between mothers and fathers.
Women talk more overall, and use higher pitch when talking to their children.
Yet, we show how female bargaining power can tilt this division of parental investment.

Men invest more in child speech, and do so in a more engaging way for the child, when their wives believe investments are important determinants of child language development and when they have higher bargaining power.

We show that these results are unlikely to be driven by assortative matching between parents, learning, or persuasion, by other characteristics of parents and children, or by unobservable village, partners, or child level differences.
Paternal investment does not simply reflect higher paternal care but specific investment that matches the mother’s beliefs.
We provide further evidence, included at the individual level, that singles out the role of female economic bargaining power from other potential dimensions of female status.

Altogether, our results suggest that more powerful women are able to elicit from their spouses 44 We focus on fathers’ costs since our results show that mothers’ costs do not influence parental investments.
As for fathers’ returns, shifting mothers’ costs has no effect on parental investments.
the investment that they believe matters.
Our findings have several implications for policy.
We show that women, when deciding on their own parental investments, act on their beliefs about returns; while men act on their costs.
Men also act on their wives’ beliefs – but only when women are powerful enough.

Consequently, our policy simulations show that lowering costs for men, for example through training about playful activities they might enjoy with their children, is effective in raising paternal investments, but modifying fathers’ beliefs, for example with information treatments on the benefits of child-directed speech, is not.

For mothers, reducing costs is not effective at changing their investments, while increasing their beliefs about returns has not only a direct effect on their own investments but may also have an indirect effect, through the elicitation of investments by their husbands, especially when coupled with women’s empowerment programs.
Indeed, the greatest gains in paternal investments would be achieved through female empowerment programs.

Overall, our results stress the importance of considering the dynamic of intra-household allocation of investments and spillovers across parents when designing and evaluating early childhood interventions.
Finally, our study demonstrates the effectiveness of a scalable and cost-efficient method for measuring paternal and maternal language inputs.
This method, coupled with our approach of measuring beliefs and costs, provides a comprehensive framework for understanding parental investment decisions.

We hope this work inspires future research to adopt similar data collection strategies, furthering our understanding of the economics of parenting and promoting rich, engaging, and fruitful parental investments in children.
